speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two of an interview with Stella Faitek on March the 6th, 1997. In the orphanage, was there a little synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had a synagogue. We had a Hebrew teacher, which was later in Israel, Yeven, he was a writer. He wrote books-- children's books in Israel. He was our teacher in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were the Hebrew classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saturday special sections. Every day in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before school, before breakfast we had to pray.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so Dr. Korczak really was, in a way, the rabbi of the-- of the orphanage.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was praying with us with a [INAUDIBLE] on the head. And then we went to the dining room to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as recreation, sports, activities in the orphanage, what-- what do you recall about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was playing with us Two Fires, it called. And then basketball he used to play with us. And he used to dance with us. He sang with us just like a child. Sometimes I looked at him, and I thought that he is behaving like a child. He danced with us. He sang with us. He was just with us together just like a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet, he was also a father.

speaker: interviewee
text: A father to all the children which loved him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- they felt that he loved them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. Every child when you went by, he touched by the shoulder. And he asked, how was your day today? It was very, very good. It was just like a mother, not like a father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Pani Stefa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pani Stefa was a little [PAUSES] not so warm as he is, but she was OK. She was strict. He was very soft to the children. She was strict, but very good. She knew what she want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- did your father visit you in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Except sometimes he used to come when we had a play. They used to come to watch the play. And except this, each child Saturday went out to the family if they have. Most of the children had a grandmother, a grandfather, a sister, a brother, because this house by Korczak, every child has to have somebody. If they haven't got anybody, they were in another home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And did your stepmother ever come to visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And meanwhile, your-- what about your two brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to come also to watch the show, which we played on the holidays. They used to come and watch it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were living with your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your stepmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not a long time-- a short time later, my brother was given-- both brothers were given away to-- one learned carpentry-- carpenter-- and the other a tailor. And they never went back to my father. By the people, they taught them how to sew or how to be a carpenter until they married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the plays that you were in? Any of the parts that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Purim, we were dressed differently. We were dancing-- Purim spiel. [PAUSES] It's hard to remember so many years ago. It's 70 years ago. [LAUGHS] But it was very pleasant. I remember this. We were active in sport. We always have been occupied.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a kind of a little-- of a little world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of a little world, yeah, and except as we felt like one family. All the children, we felt like one family. We were very close. Until now, I correspond with a friend of mine which is in Montreal. And in Israel-- I was in Israel eight times. Every time I come there, we have meetings. We see each other. And we are very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did Dr. Korczak do to make the children feel that they were part of a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: He read books to us. Then we had games which he made-- poets which wrote books. And he had cards-- how many books this poet wrote. He liked-- he liked very much Rabin-- Rab-- Rabindranath Tagore. Have you heard about the writer?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he liked very much Mickiewicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mickiewicz, I have heard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mickiewicz, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first one, Rabin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam Mickiewicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Adam Mickiewicz. But the Robin, a Polish-- was a Polish author, Robin Trenatagore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabindranath Tagore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dranitagore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabindrath Tagore.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Polish--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he wasn't a Polish. Mickiewicz was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mickiewicz was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to guess which writer wrote this book and which writer wrote this book. And we had-- we were then rewarded with postal cards. Each postal card was signed by him for playing nice the game. Then every child was rewarded if he make seven days cold showers. If the child for seven days took cold showers summertime, he got a reward the postal card signed by Dr. Korczak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the idea of the cold shower-- this was what? To save-- to save money?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: To be clean, OK, to be clean. So he emphasized cleanliness.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the other workers in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember Buchner, which was the gym teacher. Weingarten was the Hebrew teacher. And music teacher-- I remember them all. It was very interesting. And we-- I did belong to all the groups, because I like-- liked sport. I liked dancing. We had a teacher in dancing. We had everything what a child in the rich homes has, we had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know, was the Jewish community who supported the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish Joint--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Joint--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish family service from Israel-- from America. Then we had Polish organizations. Pilsudski was a member. His daughters-- I remember his daughters. They used to come to visit us-- lots of Polish organizations. Then ORT was supporting. We had lots of philanthropists there-- Dr. Eliasberg, which we have lots of doctors which used to take care of the children. And they used to send a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, uh, what did you think about Pilsudski?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very nice person. Very good to Jews. Very good to Jews. I saw him a few times. He used to come with the children. And everybody liked him. He was a different person than the other Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was-- he was very respected-- Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the children. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when he died, do you remember his--

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in time of war. No, no, he died before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, '30-- in the mid-'30s. 1935 he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: '35? Yeah. Yeah, right. Right, I will recall the date, 29th of-- he died the 29th. I don't remember which month. Yeah, you're right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think looking back on-- on the orphanage and Dr. Korczak? And you were there for six years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there from, say, 1925, maybe, until 1931? Something like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No. I was-- I went out '29. I came out '29. I was-- I went there in 1922?

speaker: interviewer
text: It could be.

speaker: interviewee
text: '23. And I went out '28.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went out '28?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came '22 or '23. And you went out '28 or '29.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so during--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 15 or 16 years old when I went  out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what do you feel that Dr. Korczak gave you that you-- that affected your life? That influenced your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave me his kindness, his understanding, his beliefs, his patience, which he had. He gave it to me. And I feel like he was mine father. He raised me. He gave me life. I liked him very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say that he gave you his beliefs, what-- what were those beliefs?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be kind. To be forgiven-- forgiveness, to help. [PAUSES] What else do we need in life?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Dr. Korczak then in the orphanage every single day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every single day and night. If he went out, he had programs in radio for children. He went out to give the referat. And he wrote books for children's books. He went out very often for meetings. He was invited to Korcha-- to Pilsudski. So he was very busy. But most of the time he was home with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember hearing him on the radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The program on the radio was titled 'Old Doctor'-- 'Old Doctor Speaks.' This was his title on the radio. I remember his books which he wrote, because all the books I was reading. And I had the books here. And I gave it I think to Barbara to the Holocaust here.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They have it in their--

speaker: interviewer
text: The local foundation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The local Holocaust foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: They they have a few books from me. They have a lot of material of Korczak's. I gave it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the books specifically?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'How to Love Children,' [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He wrote two books. One book about he also was a director in a Polish institute orphans. The book was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] was a Yiddish book. And the other book was written about the Polish institution. Then he had [NON-ENGLISH  SPEECH]. I wrote all the books. I don't remember all it-- all of it. But he was a very good writer. He was a doctor. And also he was in the army. He was with a medal from the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the First World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember he med-- wear the medals. He was somebody which will never be born again.

speaker: interviewer
text: A person who really knew how to give.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. He gave all of him. He gave everything. He was devoted to the children. He didn't have time for himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: The books-- the children's books that he wrote-- the children's books were about children living in the present time or in the past? Or do you remem-- were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: The books which he was written-- it wasn't about the orphanage. All kind of-- I remember he also had a book. The book was [INAUDIBLE]. He had a girl. He was in love with the girl, but she was much younger. And her parents didn't allow him to marry her. He wrote about her. He wrote all kinds of books. Lots of books.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the radio, he read stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: He read stories. And then he had questions and answers. All was on the tele-- on the radio. It was I think once a day he had the radio. The title was titled 'Doctor Speaks.' 'Old Doctor Speaks.'

speaker: interviewer
text: After you left the orphanage in the late 1920s, did you see Korczak again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw him very often. I didn't want to go to the-- to my father. There was-- if you went out of Korczak's house, and you didn't have where to go, he was trying to get a place. And there was a house for the youth-- teenagers. It was also for orphans. He located me there. From there, he called the art school, and I learned to sew underwear.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I finished this, he asked me, what do you want to do now? I said, I would like to work. He called up a house-- a orphan house-- and they took me there to sew underwear.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a year, since I was practicing by Korczak too, I got to work as a social worker in the house-- in the orphanage. And the ma-- and the orphanage where I used to work was 49 children, boys. And I worked there. This was my job thanks to him. And there I worked until the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you started there when you were maybe 18?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a Jewish orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was an orphanage that was run by all-- by Dr. Korczak? Or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They were different. This was run by the Gmina, the Jewish organization. But Korczak doesn't have nothing to do with this. We had lots of orphanages there. There was a Praga and an Volska, lots of it. We have also children which were thrown away. This is a separate house for the thrown away children. Korczak didn't have nothing to do with it. We had a lots of orphanages.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the orphanage that you worked in was only for boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only for boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- these were from broken homes, or from-- with no parents, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Broken homes. Most of it broken homes-- poor people. And from this house, I took out the two children, which I showed you on the picture, with me when I came back to Poland. And I located them in Bialystok in an orphanage, because they were young. They were at that time I think 10, 11. And they were there in the orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, um, kind of work, then, did you do in the orphanage as a social worker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, took care of them-- of their lessons, of their house-- homework when they came back from school. We went together summertime for swimming. We went to the movies. Then we took care by the dining room. After this, we had every child had the obligation to do something. So I watched them what they are doing to do the good job. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your experience with Dr. Korczak influence how you acted or related to the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have the knowledge, because he gave me a good lesson. And I knew what to do the right thing. I had very many boys. They had fights.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one time, I punished the group. And they were very mad at me. And at that time, I was supposed to go for a vacation. And I chose a vacation in the mountains skiing. And one of the boys in the other group asked me, where are you going for a vacation? I said, I'm going to the mountains skiing. And he said, well, I wish you to fall all the mountains and to break your legs.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are open. A girl will never say it. A boy is always open. I like to work with boys. It was very, very good experience. And my experience helped me to raise the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later, did you-- was-- did the boy who said that become friendly to you? Or he's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they forget. They forget very fast. It's a moment, because I punish them. And they thought that I wasn't good to the other group. But they-- they were good.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the early 1930s in Poland came the Depression. Do you remember, was there-- were there shortages of food? Of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Korczak's home. I don't remember this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And not in the orphanage where-- where you worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. No. No. We had everything. The Jewish organization, philanthropist-- we had lots of philanthropists. We were never hungry by Korczak. And the children never were hungry on-- in my house. It was a small home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived there really for maybe six years or seven years, something like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the war. Till The war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, before we married. I married 1939, April. So a month earlier, I stopped working.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: In sport club.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what-- what kind of a sport club?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH  SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: A Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Polish, yeah. A Jewish Polish. But I belonged mostly to Maccabi. And I knew his brother more than I knew him. But I married him '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: By that time, you had become-- you were also active politically?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was-- I belonged to a organization-- a communistic organization, MOP-- Jewish youth. And I believed in it, because I thought that communism is straight for everybody. There is no poor people. Everybody is strai-- straight. And this, I liked it. But when I came to Russia, I was cured. They cured me completely. I saw what does it mean, communism. Lies. And it was a different world for me then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, was Dr. Korczak political in any way? Did he ever discuss politics?

speaker: interviewee
text: He hated communism. [PAUSES] I-- he left Israel. He was twice in Israel. Stefa was in Israel too before the war. When the broke-- the war broke out, she ran back to Poland, because she said that she was staying with my girlfriend in Israel. And she said to her, I have to go back home. The children are waiting for me. I think this war will broke out. She came back to Poland. And she went to the ovens together with Dr. Korczak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- you were-- in the-- in Poland you were a-- a communist. But were you also a Zionist?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in Korczak's home, I wasn't a communist. When I went out, I had a girlfriend which she belonged to the organization. And she pulled me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into the communist youth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But when I was by Korczak, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, um, but in the Maccabi-- you were in the Maccabi Sports Club, which was a Zionist sports club. Was it just be-- just to be in a sports club?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sport.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't that you were a Zionist at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I come to the Maccabi, it wasn't a communistic organization. Later.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But even so, Maccabi was a sports club. To me, it was everything sport. But when they planned to make the Makabijada in Israel, as a communist, I was against it for Spartakiada in Russia. And I had worked against the Makabijada for Spartakiada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a-- in Russia, there was a kind of another kind of Maccabi. It was called Sportkiaba?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spartakiada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spartakiada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spartakiada. This was in time before the war. So they catched me on the work, on the anti-Zionist. And they threw me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the only one to go to Makabijada without money, because I was the captain of the volleyball and basketball. And I was the best player. So I was rewarded with to go without paying a penny. And they gave me ultimatum. You sign that you are not a communist, and you will go to Israel. And I said, I cannot sign it. It's I am not still a. I am still a. Then they said, then you will not go. And I decided to go. And I talk it over with the one from the organization. He said, you cannot sign it. If you will sign it, you will be stamped as a [? opportunist. ?] And you might be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a communist who told you.

speaker: interviewee
text: A communist, yes. I didn't want to be killed. And I didn't go to the Makabijada. This what wrecked mine life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was dreaming of going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you think because if you had gone to Israel, you might have stayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything-- everything would be changed. Who knows what would be? Most of the people which went there, 65% stayed there. Not everybody came back to Poland. It was before the war. It was 1934 or '35. Lots of people were standing there. Mine life would be different. So, but I had a good lesson there. They cured me completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to Russia eventually. But in the-- in the communist youth group, were there many Jews? Or were there-- was it half and half of Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lots of Jews. Lots of Jews. We had partially groups. 10 in a group, 8 a group. I didn't see big groups. Only in demonstrations. We had demonstrations on the streets were big groups. But otherwise, little groups, 10, 8 in a group. So we weren't in a big--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never experienced any anti-Semitism there in the communist youth group? There was no anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to look, probably. I-- I don't remember this. I felt lots of communism-- uh, of anti-Semitism on the streets, in the papers. A Jew couldn't get a job even to be a janitor. It was very hard to get a good job. This I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any physical assaults? Any physical anti-Semitism? Anybody attacking you or somebody close to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard-- I saw in the paper lots of things. But [PAUSES] I didn't pay much attention to it. I was busy with myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- did you-- did you ever think, why is there so much anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. Now, I understand. But at that time, I didn't understand why. They just didn't like Jews. They didn't like other nationalities. I didn't pay attention to it.
